data.title: China’s chief space contractor readies plans for tourism programme
data.description:
As companies like SpaceX and Blue Origin make headlines for sending non-astronauts into space on pricey private tours, a Chinese state firm is expected to unveil its own space tourism programme this week, joining other domestic enterprises as they attempt to make headway in the rapidly emerging – and US-dominated – market.
China Aerospace Science and Technology Corp (CASC), the primary contractor for the country's space programme, will unveil details of its inaugural tour at the China Hi-tech Fair, which opens on Friday in the southern tech hub of Shenzhen.
According to the Securities Times, a state-owned financial newspaper, reusable rockets and spacecraft will catapult passengers to the edge of the atmosphere, with each paying a hefty fee for the out-of-this-world experience; ticket prices have been estimated to start at 1 million yuan (US$140,534).
The trip will bring tourists about 100km (62 miles) above sea level – passing the internationally recognised boundary of outer space known as the Karman line – allowing them to feel weightlessness and view the Earth.
CASC did not immediately reply to an inquiry from the Post for comment.
China has sped up the pace of its rocket and satellite launches this year, sending off four this week alone, with the value of its commercial space activities projected to hit 2.5 trillion yuan (US$351.3 billion) in 2025, Securities Times reported on Wednesday.
As the country plays catch-up with the more developed space programmes of the US and Russia, its aerospace operators are doing the same with the largely American firms that have sent wealthy individuals well beyond the Karman line.


SpaceX and Blue Origin, respectively founded by billionaires Elon Musk and Jeff Bezos, have already chartered more than a dozen suborbital and low-orbit flights. A SpaceX crew completed the first commercial spacewalk in 2024, and the company has sent tourists to the International Space Station since 2022.
Based on the projected costs, however, the trips offered by CASC appear likely to be a great deal cheaper than what the American firms charge. Prices for SpaceX and Blue Origin flights vary and are not made public, but estimates run as high as US$55 million per person for a multi-day low-orbit tour.
The Chinese state company will also face competition from domestic rivals. In October 2024, the private firm Deep Blue Aerospace announced it had already sold two tickets – at 1.5 million yuan each – for seats on a suborbital rocket ride scheduled for 2027.
Deep Blue said its first passengers would experience a combined 12 minutes of ascent and weightlessness at the edge of space, but dozens of trials would be conducted before that mission launches.
This May, a Chinese engineer in his 80s became the oldest prospective passenger for future space rides offered by a Beijing-based private firm, with the first flight slated for 2028.
And in August, the government of Guangdong province announced plans to expand its commercial space sector, pledging support for companies to build satellite constellations and hold tests for space mining and tourism.
Ma Xiaoqi, deputy president of Space Circling, a start-up based in the northwestern province of Shaanxi, said as China’s companies begin their charters, they will be able to compete with their American rivals.
“The entire commercial space market is pursuing high-reliability, low-cost reusable and high-thrust liquid rocket engines,” Ma said. “For space tourism, more launches by Chinese state-owned and private firms will bring down costs and popularise technologies in a growing ecosystem that is already as vibrant as the one in the US.”

data.title: Seoul’s backing of UN resolution on North Korea’s rights abuses tests limits of dialogue push
data.description:
South Korea has thrown its weight behind a United Nations resolution condemning the North’s dire human rights record, a choice that could complicate Seoul’s push to reopen dialogue with Pyongyang, analysts have warned.
The decision to co-sponsor the annual UN resolution – despite expectations that President Lee Jae-myung’s liberal government might step back to create a friendlier atmosphere for engagement – is likely to trigger an angry reaction from Pyongyang and narrow the already limited space for diplomacy, according to experts.
“Pyongyang is likely to react angrily to this resolution again, and it will certainly have negative impacts on Seoul’s efforts to bring North Korea back to the table,” Yang Moo-jin, a political-science professor at the University of North Korean Studies, told This Week in Asia.
North Korea has consistently and strongly opposed annual UN resolutions addressing its human rights record.

In 2024, Pyongyang condemned the UN Human Rights Council’s adoption of a resolution denouncing its abuses as “nothing but a politically motivated fraud document”.
The council had passed the resolution for the 22nd consecutive year during its 55th regular session in Geneva in 2024, denouncing the North’s human rights violations and urging all states to respect the principle of non-refoulement.
Kim Son-gyong, vice-minister for international organisations at North Korea’s foreign ministry, at that time decried the resolution as a “grave infringement upon sovereignty” and “an act of interference in internal affairs”.
Despite South Korea’s push for renewed engagement with Pyongyang, Lee’s liberal government followed the precedent set by the conservative administration of impeached former president Yoon Suk-yeol by supporting this year’s UN resolution.
According to the document distributed earlier this month, South Korea was among the 41 UN member states that co-sponsored the measure.
Analysts had widely expected that, unlike the Yoon administration’s hardline approach, the Lee government might abstain to keep open the possibility of dialogue.
South Korea had co-sponsored the resolution from 2008 to 2018 but abstained between 2019 and 2021 under former president Moon Jae-in’s liberal government amid a period of high-level diplomacy, including the 2018 summit between North Korean leader Kim Jong-un and US President Donald Trump.

Reports suggest that some within the government argued for caution, concerned that co-sponsoring the resolution could escalate tensions with Pyongyang.
However, the view that human rights constitute a universal value that should be upheld on principle ultimately prevailed.
A foreign ministry official told local media: “Our government believes it is important to bring about tangible improvements in North Korea’s human rights situation, and on that basis, we have decided to join the international community in supporting the resolution.”
Cho Han-bum, a senior researcher at the Korea Institute for National Unification, said Seoul’s decision reflected Lee’s pragmatic approach.
“The Yoon government took a hardline stance that was often too provocative, while the Lee administration is seeking to uphold universal human rights values without excessively provoking the North,” Cho said.
Notably, the United States, which had consistently co-sponsored the resolution in the past, did not take part this year – a reflection of what observers described as the Trump administration’s isolationist trend, aligned with its decision to withdraw from the UN Human Rights Council in February.
The latest UN resolution on North Korea’s human rights situation will be introduced at the UN Third Committee before being forwarded to the General Assembly plenary next month for final adoption.

Meanwhile, the Group of Seven (G7) foreign ministers on Wednesday reaffirmed their commitment to the “complete denuclearisation” of North Korea while condemning its continued weapons development.
“We strongly condemn the DPRK’s nuclear and ballistic missile programmes and reaffirm our commitment to the complete denuclearisation of the DPRK, in accordance with relevant UN Security Council resolutions,” they said in a joint statement.
DPRK is short for the Democratic People’s Republic of Korea, the North’s official name.
The statement was issued following a meeting of the G7 foreign ministers – representing the US, Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Britain, Japan and the European Union – in Niagara-on-the-Lake, Ontario, earlier this week.
The G7 ministers also expressed “grave concerns” over North Korea’s cryptocurrency thefts and urged Pyongyang to resolve the long-standing abductions issue “expeditiously”.
“International pressure for denuclearisation remains ineffective, as China and Russia – the North’s key backers – are increasingly reluctant to implement sanctions amid great-power rivalry and the ongoing war in Ukraine,” Yang noted.
In a related development, recent satellite imagery reportedly indicates that North Korea may be preparing for another large-scale military parade ahead of the Ninth Party Congress scheduled for January next year.
According to NK Pro, citing Planet Labs imagery, an increasing number of troop trucks have appeared around the replica Kim Il-sung Square at the Mirim military parade training base in southeastern Pyongyang this month.
The coming congress is expected to review the regime’s achievements over the past five years – including its strengthened nuclear deterrent – and announce new policy goals.
“Despite receiving economic assistance from Russia in return for its participation in the war in Ukraine, there has been no tangible improvement in people’s livelihoods,” Cho said.
“Under such grim circumstances, military parades remain virtually the only spectacle available to North Koreans.”

data.title: How M. Night Shyamalan’s Unbreakable redefined the superhero movie 25 years ago
data.description:
This is the latest instalment in our From the Vault feature series, in which we reflect on culturally significant movies celebrating notable anniversaries.
M. Night Shyamalan has a knack for making genre films feel real. His 1999 breakthrough, The Sixth Sense, grounded a ghost story in an anxious family drama. Its immense financial success gave him the freedom to do whatever he wanted next.
His next project was Unbreakable, which turns 25 this month. It placed a familiar superhero origin story into a real-world context, years before Christopher Nolan’s Dark Knight trilogy famously perfected the approach.
Although it underperformed at the box office, Unbreakable has since been reassessed, with figures such as Quentin Tarantino calling it “a brilliant retelling of the Superman mythology”. Viewed today, as superhero films swamp the box office, it looks closer to a masterpiece than a misstep.


When David Dunn (Bruce Willis) survives a train crash that kills the other 131 passengers, he and his son Joseph (Spencer Treat Clark) begin to suspect he might be invulnerable.
It is a theory shared by comic-book obsessive and gallery owner Elijah Price (Samuel L. Jackson), who has the opposite problem – his brittle bone disease has earned him the nickname Mr Glass.
As David and his wife Audrey (Robin Wright) try to rebuild their life together, Price pushes him to prove his powers. But is there a more sinister agenda?
Shyamalan wrote the script on spec, selling it to Disney for a record US$5 million. He hoped to reunite Pulp Fiction stars Willis and Jackson, who had a chance meeting in Morocco before being officially cast.

Jackson recalls: “Bruce went, ‘Sam, there’s this guy who’s writing a script for us.’ And he called M. Night on the phone, and Night said to me, ‘This is amazing. I’m writing one of your scenes right now.’”
Until the awkward last act, the screenplay is first-rate, full of clever details and subtle foreshadowing. As a child, Price is given comic books by his mother (Charlayne Woodard). “They say this one has a surprise ending,” she says, in a nod to fans of The Sixth Sense.
Unbreakable is its own entity – a sober, almost downbeat affair. Willis gives the most soulful performance of his career, with Clark and Wright providing moving support, evoking a sense of real lives pulled apart by forces beyond their control.
“Any time I wrote a scene that was too comic-bookish, I yanked it out,” Shyamalan said.

This is exemplified in a pivotal moment where Joseph almost shoots David to prove his invulnerability. Elegantly written and poignantly performed, it finishes with an exhausted laugh when Audrey warns, “No shooting friends, Joseph!”
Eduardo Serra’s cinematography is beautiful throughout, with each frame carefully designed to make the film appear like a live-action comic book. In one scene, post-crash, David chats to a doctor in the background, while the only other survivor bleeds out in front.
“There was a philosophy behind all the shots in the movie,” Shyamalan said.
Price is often reflected in screens or mirrors, and surrounded by flashes of regal purple. Dunn, meanwhile, inhabits a murky, blue-green world, his long raincoat standing in for a superhero’s cape as he gradually comes to accept his fate.

Critics who found the film – or Shyamalan’s films in general – a touch too self-important were given a ready-made rejoinder. “This is an art gallery, my friend,” Glass tells a customer buying an original comic-book drawing. “And this is a piece of art.”
After a harrowing sequence in which Dunn saves a family imprisoned in their own home, the final reveal – that Price is the supervillain to Dunn’s superhero and is responsible for the train crash, among other disasters – feels rushed; the set-up for a sequel that never seemed to come.
Over the years, the fan demand grew. “It just kept growing and growing until I would cross the street and invariably someone would come up and say: ‘Unbreakable! I love it, man. When are you making the sequel?’” Shyamalan said.
Never one to do as expected, Shyamalan’s eventual follow-up was 2016’s Split, which focused on a completely different character: a kidnapper with dissociative identity disorder called Kevin Wendell Crumb (James McAvoy).

That film ends – spoiler alert – with a shot of Dunn, revealing that he and Crumb occupy the same universe. Yet the seeds were sown way back in 2000.
A blink-and-you’ll-miss-it scene in Unbreakable shows a young boy and his mother walking past Dunn, who gives them a long, thoughtful look. That boy, Shyamalan would later confirm, was Crumb.
The filmmaker then completed the surprise trilogy with 2019’s inferior Glass, which brought McAvoy, Willis and Jackson together.
Say what you like about Shyamalan’s later, more divisive works, but there is something so bold about setting up a sequel 16 years in advance; it is almost as if he had some secret superpowers of his own.

Want more articles like this? Follow SCMP Film on Facebook

data.title: National Games: veteran Hong Kong equestrian Nicole Pearson desperate for one more medal
data.description:
Evergreen Hong Kong equestrian Nicole Pearson said she was “desperate” to claim one more major medal to “justify the ridiculousness of my life”.
Pearson and fellow event riders Thomas Ho and Su Yuxuan endured a difficult first day of the National Games competition in Shenzhen.
Games debutant Su is 16th following the opening dressage discipline, with Ho and Pearson 22nd and 24th, respectively. Hong Kong are sixth in the team standings. The cross-country and jumping rounds are set for Friday and Saturday.
Recalling her team bronze from the 2014 Asian Games, when Hong Kong recovered from a sluggish start, Pearson, who was acquainted with competition horse Angel only one week ago, said: “On paper, it does not look great, it certainly wasn’t the best dressage I’ve ridden, but I’ve done enough to realise anything is possible.
“Championships throw up things you never thought could happen. The three of us are very tight … we need a bit of luck, but I’m optimistic we’ll do a good job.”

As a rider whose major competition experience dates back to the 1986 Asian Games in Seoul, Pearson joked that she asked herself every day why she was still competing.
“It boils down to loving the horses and the sport,” Pearson, 58, said.
“Every year, I struggle a bit more with fitness, injuries and the state of my body … every time I sit on a horse, the pain management is more challenging, and my ability’s not what it was. I’m not as supple or flexible.
“But I have a couple of horses in England that are among the best I’ve ridden and that keeps you going. It makes no sense to a sane and sensible person, but I’m neither sane nor sensible.”
Pearson said she had been regularly toing and froing from her New Forest home over the past few months, in advance of these Games and next month’s Asian Championships in Thailand.
“My Cathay [Pacific] points have increased,” Pearson said. “Preparation probably hasn’t been ideal, but I’ll get on with it. I am desperate for one more medal; it will justify the ridiculousness of my life.”

While teammate Ho said walking into the Shenzhen Guangming International Equestrian Centre had been “a daunting experience, it hits you quite hard and is overwhelming for the horses”, Pearson said the atmosphere had been “the least of my worries”.
“I remember in Shanghai for the 1997 National Games, they bussed in a rent-a-crowd who had never seen an equestrian event,” she said. “They were brilliant and participated, but not always at the appropriate time.”
Su, meanwhile, said he had experienced “a tough morning … I had expected quite a lot, so it’s hard not to be slightly disappointed”.
The 28-year-old added that the atmosphere had “sparked up” his mount Jones JL.
“He got quite tense, I tried to eke out the points I could and we did a pretty good job,” Su said. “I’m only seven points from the leader [Liang Ruiji of Guangdong] so it’s all to play for.”
Compared with the Asian Games, where he competed in 2014 and 2018, Su said the National Games were “on a different level in terms of their importance, and the expectation and pressure”.

Also based in England, Su said it had been “so special to get a call-up for this and compete close to home”.
“A couple of friends have come across the border to watch, so it’s nice knowing they’re there,” he said.
Ho, 36, meanwhile, was targeting flawless cross-country and jumping efforts on Opalien Van ’T Gebergte to move into podium contention.
“It’s just the beginning. My horse is young but she held her nerve,” he said. “I was a bit disappointed with the scoring, but that’s competition.
“In equestrian, we don’t normally have the chance to compete as a team. It’s nice to talk tactics and help and support each other.”

data.title: China must not be sold latest cutting-edge technology, says former US commerce secretary
data.description:
One of the architects of former US President Joe Biden’s tech export controls warned on Thursday that Washington needs better coordination with allies to deny China access to high-end chips and cutting-edge products, criticising a plan by US President Donald Trump’s administration to allow sales of certain Nvidia chips.
Citing Beijing’s massive spending on efforts to establish China’s technological leadership, former commerce secretary Gina Raimondo said in a panel discussion at the Council on Foreign Relations that the US government should do it in a more precise way to achieve a balance between corporate and economic security interests.
“Export controls are like a very powerful tool in the toolkit that the US government has. And I think they should be used with their allies in a precise way to deny China the most cutting-edge technology that we have, that they don’t,” she said.
“I believe now we have better, more sophisticated chips and we shouldn’t allow them to have it at the end of the day.”


Raimondo made the comments when asked about the resumption of Nvidia’s H20 chip sales to China in July. The Trump administration granted export licenses for some Nvidia chips to China in exchange for 15 per cent of the revenues, a move that ran aground after Beijing stepped up national security scrutiny of the product.
Meanwhile, discussions arose over whether the US export control could accelerate China’s investment and eventually American firms such as Nvidia could lose their China market share.
In a talk hosted by Citadel Securities in early October, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang mentioned that its China AI GPU market share went from 95 per cent to zero.
Also, the extension of the US export control list, as the affiliate rule announced by the US Commerce Department in late September, led to China’s further rare earth retaliation measures.
“Denying US companies revenue is a bad thing. That’s why it has to be used in a precise way, and it’s hard,” said Raimondo.
The former commerce secretary spearheaded the designation of the 2022 Chips Act and the high-end chip sales ban on China. She also promoted the Chip 4 strategy, an alliance with South Korea, Japan and Taiwan for semiconductor supply chain security.
Although Raimondo claimed to be a firm believer that the US should play offensively and focus more on advancing its own technological strength, she noted that export controls are vital for national security.
“America’s national security should never be for sale. America’s national security shouldn’t be compromised,” she said.
An economic security task force co-chaired by Raimondo and Justin G. Muzinich, deputy secretary of the Treasury in 2018-2021, found that US advantages in artificial intelligence, quantum and biotechnology are increasingly contested.
“Whoever in whatever administration, I think the US needs to step up its game in this effort, in a bipartisan way, to be much more proactive reaching out to those allies and countries who themselves want to wean [themselves] from the risk of China,” Raimondo warned.
“We have to be more proactive and strategic and have a fulsome approach to go to those countries and structure agreements with our private sector.”
US President Trump already said he wants Nvidia’s advanced Blackwell chips for US companies only. “We don’t give [the Blackwell] chip to other people,” he said in a CBS interview a week ago.
Nividia confirmed subsequently that there are no plans to ship Blackwell GPUs to China.

data.title: Bananas and beef: White House unveils Latin America trade deals
data.description:
The White House on Thursday announced trade agreements with Argentina, Guatemala, Ecuador and El Salvador, the latest deals since US President Donald Trump imposed sweeping tariffs around the world.
Bananas and coffee are among the groceries whose prices could fall for stretched US consumers, it said, amid growing discontent about the cost of living under Trump’s second presidency.
The four countries, all in America’s backyard in South and Central America, agreed to open their markets to US products while Washington grants tariff relief on some items in return, the White House said.
“We believe that these agreements are going to help achieve balanced trade, achieve reciprocity, and reduce the deficits in the long run,” a senior US administration official told reporters.
“Our expectation is that there’ll be some positive effects for prices, for things like coffee, cocoa, bananas.”

The deals are also a reward for leaders that are friendly towards the Trump administration.
They include beef-producing Argentina, whose libertarian president Javier Milei visited the White House last month, and El Salvador’s Nayib Bukele, the self-styled “world’s coolest dictator”.
“Friends,” Bukele said in a post on social media, along with a copy of a joint statement with the United States.
Guatemalan President Bernardo Arevalo – whose country signed a deal with the Trump administration earlier this year to take back migrants – hailed his country’s “strong relationship” with Washington.
“After months of intensive work and frank dialogue with the United States government, we became one of the first countries in the world to reach an agreement to reduce and eliminate tariffs,” he said in a statement.

data.title: Stick with emerging-market stocks, especially in China and AI, UBS advises
data.description:
Emerging-market (EM) equities are likely to deliver slower returns next year after an expected period of early outperformance, but investors should stay overweight on Chinese stocks thanks to attractive valuations, solid fund flows and favourable macro catalysts, according to UBS.
After growth of about 18 per cent in earnings per share (EPS) in 2025, the companies in the MSCI Emerging Markets Index would deliver earnings growth of 15 per cent in 2026 and 10 per cent in 2027, with artificial intelligence “playing a significant role”, said Sunil Tirumalai, head of EM and Asia equity strategy at UBS Global Research, in a report this week.
The Swiss investment bank said EM markets still offered multiple opportunities, supported by a “normalising” macro environment in 2026. This included a rebound in global growth, visible fiscal support in developed markets and Federal Reserve balance-sheet expansion, which is expected to keep long-end US yields in check, easing some of the yield headwinds for emerging-market equities, according to UBS.
AI remained a key driver of EM performance and would stay healthy given solid cash flows, low leverage among big spenders and relatively attractive valuations, UBS said. It added that about 23 per cent of the MSCI EM index comprised companies with direct revenue exposure to AI demand, but these names accounted for more than 42 per cent of 2025 returns and drove almost all of the EPS upgrades this year.
These companies’ earnings growth was expected to remain above 20 per cent in 2026 before normalising in 2027, according to the bank. UBS preferred Chinese consumer-facing AI names over Korean and Taiwanese peers, given more reasonable valuations and less aggressive re-rating.
The bank reiterated its overweight stance on China versus India, citing supportive signs such as easing tariff risks and growing AI revenue.
The report came amid a bull run in mainland shares, with the CSI 300 Index rising more than 20 per cent so far this year. A separate UBS report on Friday said that global institutional funds narrowed their underweight position on China to 1.3 per cent from 1.6 per cent during the latest quarter, with global, EM and Asia mandates all adding modestly to their exposure.
Holdings in Chinese equities among the top 40 global investors rose to 1.1 per cent in the third quarter, the report said. This was the highest level since the first quarter of 2023, but was still well below the nearly 2 per cent peak recorded in 2020.
Elsewhere, UBS adjusted several country ratings, upgrading Mexico to neutral due to resilient US demand, steady foreign direct investment and reasonable valuations. Thailand was downgraded to underweight as one of the most expensive markets in the region, while the Philippines was cut to neutral amid cooling economic momentum and policy uncertainty tied to corruption investigations.

data.title: As the US sits out Cop30, China and others must pick up the slack
data.description:
Real actions beyond speeches are what the world’s leaders are being asked to come up with at Cop30. Host Brazil’s urgent call, cast into focus at the first UN Conference of the Parties set in the heart of the threatened Amazon, is a tall order. Carbon emissions remain too high to meet global warming targets and the US government is a no show.
But some have found hope in a recent push to form a “coalition of the willing”. Such technology and investment partnerships, primarily among developing nations, could reinvigorate efforts that have long depended on handouts from developed countries.
This coalition would benefit from China’s expanding role in powering green economy transformations. Chinese commitments to foreign clean energy manufacturing and infrastructure projects since 2022 have exceeded US$200 billion, according to one estimate. Adjusted for inflation, that is more than US Marshall Plan spending to rebuild Europe after World War II.
China’s global role in green tech expansion is likely to continue. There is intense domestic competition among makers of electric vehicles, solar panels, wind turbines and batteries. Companies will find eager markets in developing nations. Many are linked to Beijing’s Belt and Road Initiative, which has helped build a China-centred trading network.
Brazilian President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva said leaders should make the Amazon gathering a “Cop of truth” that shows a real shared commitment to Earth. One avenue being explored is the Tropical Forests Forever Facility to protect rainforests.
China has shown support for the fund in principle. It has not invested, citing “common but differentiated responsibilities”. The concept holds that rich nations built prosperity on fossil fuels and should bear the primary burden for financing and emissions cuts.
Such engagement seems unlikely from Washington. US President Donald Trump skipped the Amazon gathering and sent no delegation. His political opponent, California Governor Gavin Newsom, was there – but unable to take part in official negotiations.
Brazil, which also hosted the 1992 Earth Summit that gave rise to the annual Cop gatherings, has shown an admirable level of engagement. Brazilian President Lula said Cop30 risks ending up as another showcase of good ideas, and he voiced hope it would instead provide “contact with reality” and “effective action” to tackle climate change.
Even if Washington sits it out, the conference gatherings are important. Climate change continues despite denials and political wrangling. China and other nations, large and small, must take action to pick up the slack.

data.title: 5 new afternoon teas in Hong Kong, from scent-inspired to chocolate overload
data.description:
Afternoon tea is a popular pastime in Hong Kong, and the ritual of savouring small treats is deeply ingrained in the city’s culinary landscape.
For many, the tradition – a legacy of British colonial rule – offers a moment of reprieve from the city’s hectic pace, a tradition that has evolved into a social event enjoyed by locals and tourists alike.
If you fancy a few hours indulging in tiny sweet and savoury delights, then these new themed afternoon tea sets – from a chocolate celebration courtesy of Belgian chocolatier Godiva to a feast of cheese – should help get you in the festive mood.
The Butterfly Room’s autumn afternoon tea












View this post on Instagram

























This year, Rosewood Hong Kong won the world’s best afternoon tea award by La Liste, so you know you are in for something special with its autumn afternoon tea (HK$568 per person), available until the end of November.
Feast on butternut squash quiche and chestnut velouté with black truffle foam, and sandwiches including potted crab, shimeji mushroom, Scottish salmon with Granny Smith, and eggs and ham. On the sweet side, freshly baked treats such as Hokkaido milk tea flan, scones, vanilla brioche and wild berry cheesecake complete the set.
The experience, complemented by a curated pairing from French gourmet tea company Mariage Freres or a choice of freshly brewed coffee, is available from Monday to Friday (2.30pm-7pm, last orders at 6pm) and Saturday, Sunday and public holidays (midday-7pm, last orders at 6pm).
Rosewood Hong Kong, Victoria Dockside, 18 Salisbury Road, Tsim Sha Tsui, Kowloon. Tel: 3891 8888. Book here
Green x Godiva: Starlit Carnival

Hotel Icon’s Green restaurant is getting into a sweet festive mood in a collaboration with Belgian chocolatier Godiva. From November 17 to February 13, 2026, the menu will feature dishes infused with the rich essence of Godiva, starting with a selection of four exquisite appetisers – smoked salmon rose with blinis and Godiva chocolate powder, mini mozzarella cheese ball with cherry tomato, deep-fried oysters with Godiva chocolate mayonnaise, and teriyaki eel and egg mousse on toasted brioche.
The show-stopping centrepiece is a shimmering crystal-globe Godiva dessert stand. Inside, a carousel slowly turns, presenting artisanal confections such as matcha and roasted rice choux, Japanese sesame Godiva chocolate cheesecake, raspberry Godiva chocolate panna cotta, chocolate-dipped strawberry and yuzu chocolate tart with lemon verbena.
Hotel Icon, 17 Science Museum Road, Tsim Sha Tsui. Tel: 3400 1300. Book here
Ebb & Flow x International Fragrance Association

The International Fragrance Association, in collaboration with The Park Lane Hong Kong hotel, presents the Ebb & Flow x International Fragrance Association Scents & Savours Afternoon Tea (HK$268 for one/HK$528 for two).
Uniting six fragrance brands – Anna Sui, Clean, Coach, Dolce & Gabbana Beauty, Furla and Kate Spade – with the hotel’s culinary team, the set features delicacies inspired by the signature notes of each fragrance.
They include citrus scallop ceviche, seared duck foie gras on ciabatta toast, and pink peppercorn-crusted Wagyu beef burger.
Sweets include a bergamot cream and jasmine panna cotta, a mandarin jelly and cardamom, Granny Smith apple and orange blossom custard cream tart, matcha mousse and rose and citrus and verbena scone with clotted cream and jam. Available on Saturday, Sunday and public holidays from 3pm-5pm.
The Park Lane Hong Kong, 310 Gloucester Road, Causeway Bay. Tel: 2839 3377. Book here
Lobster Brunost Cheese Fondue

The Luxe Manor hotel in Tsim Sha Tsui has joined forces with century-old Norwegian cheese brand Tine to present a fondue afternoon tea (HK$588 for two) at Finds.
The set features Tine’s Norwegian Brunost caramel cheese that has been blended into a lobster and pumpkin soup, and served with a selection of Nordic seafood and seasonal vegetables for dipping.
Available from December 1 until January 31, 2026, from 3pm-5.30pm. Book on or before December 22 for 25 per cent off.
The Luxe Manor, 39 Kimberley Road, Tsim Sha Tsui, Kowloon. Tel: 2522 9318. Book here
Cheese Please from Cruise x Sbakery by Mama Soo

Cruise Restaurant & Bar and local brand Sbakery by Mama Soo have joined forces to dish up an afternoon tea set (HK$688 for two) that celebrates all things cheese.
Available on weekends and public holidays from December 6 to March 1, 2026, the set includes Sbakery’s bestselling apple crumble mini cheesecake and chocolate hazelnut cheesecake, along with its popular cheesecake cookies in New York chocolate chip and sea salt and spiced caramel white chocolate flavours.
Other cheese-tastic treats include marinated watermelon with dried sweet fish floss and Parmesan cheese, black truffle and cheese toastie, lobster cappuccino and escargot en croûte with aged Cheddar cheese, along with all-you-can-eat soufflé pancakes with cheesecake foam and seasonal ice cream flavours.
Cruise Restaurant & Bar, 23/F, West Tower, Hyatt Centric Victoria Harbour Hong Kong, 1 North Point Estate Lane, North Point. Tel: 3896 9898. Book here

data.title: Trump administration sues California over new electoral maps
data.description:
US President Donald Trump’s administration sued California on Thursday over its new redistricting maps after a ballot measure adopting new congressional districts passed in the state last week.
The measure could give the Democratic-led state five more seats in the US House of Representatives and was intended as a counterweight to Republican efforts to give their party more congressional seats in Texas and elsewhere amid a push by Trump.
The Justice Department intervened as a plaintiff in a November 5 lawsuit by the California Republican Party and 19 registered voters in the state. The case challenges California’s ballot initiative Proposition 50, which allows temporary use of new congressional district maps.
US Attorney General Pam Bondi in a statement said: “California’s redistricting scheme is a brazen power grab that tramples on civil rights and mocks the democratic process.”
A spokesperson for California Governor Gavin Newsom said, in response to the lawsuit, “these losers lost at the ballot box and soon they will also lose in court”.

Newsom in a prior statement said Californians had been “uniquely targeted” by Trump and that the redistricting would provide “much-needed accountability to Trump’s efforts to undermine the democratic process”.
In August, Republican Texas Governor Greg Abbott signed a new Republican-backed map into law that was aimed at flipping five Democratic-held seats.
Civil rights groups have sued over the new Texas map, claiming it reduces the electoral power of minority voters.
The Republican-led lawsuit in California alleged that the state’s redistricting effort violates provisions of the US Constitution. It said California illegally drew new congressional district lines based on race to favour Hispanic voters.
The Justice Department in its complaint said California’s map “manipulates district lines in the name of bolstering the voting power of Hispanic Californians because of their race”.
